examples.title: DFS Traversal Implementation
examples.description: Recursive Depth First Search traversal of a graph using adjacency list
examples.explanation: DFS uses recursion to explore as deep as possible before backtracking. The visited array prevents processing vertices twice and handles cycles.

examples.title: BFS Traversal Implementation
examples.description: Breadth First Search traversal using queue for level-order exploration
examples.explanation: BFS uses a queue to process vertices in order of their distance from the source. Vertices are explored level by level, making it suitable for finding shortest paths in unweighted graphs.

examples.title: Adjacency Matrix Operations
examples.description: C program demonstrating graph operations using adjacency matrix representation
examples.explanation: Adjacency matrix provides O(1) edge lookup. Adding an edge is simply setting matrix[u][v] = 1. Suitable for dense graphs but wastes space for sparse graphs.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for edge operations, O(V²) for initialization

examples.title: Finding Connected Components
examples.description: Algorithm to find all connected components using DFS
examples.explanation: To find connected components, we run DFS starting from unvisited vertices. Each new DFS start marks a new component. This is useful for network analysis and cluster identification.

examples.title: Adjacency List with Generic Types
examples.description: Java implementation of adjacency list for graph storage and operations
examples.explanation: Generic adjacency list implementation allows any type as vertex. Uses HashMap for vertex lookup and LinkedList for storing neighbors. Efficient for sparse graphs.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) add vertex, O(1) add edge, O(degree) isAdjacent
